Chapter 2
Introduction
Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) in scientific domains is very different from general gram-
mar correction.Scientific and engineering papers contain many technical terms, detailed sen-
tencestructures,andelementslikeequationsorLaTeXcommandsthatarenotpartofnor-
mal language.Scientific and engineering manuscripts exhibit a much higher concentration of
domain-specificterminology,complexsyntacticstructures,andnon-naturallanguagetokens.
Theseincludemathematicalequations,chemicalexpressions,algorithmicpseudo-code,fig-
ure/table references, and structured citation formats (e.g., IEEE, ACM). Such constructs carry
high semantic load and must be preserved verbatim. Any unintended modification risks altering
experimental claims, mathematical correctness, or reproducibility.
General-purpose GEC models, typically trained on learner corpora or web text, optimize for
fluency gains and penalize scientific writing characteristics such as passive voice, nominaliza-
tions, and discontinuous noun phrases.These stylistic patterns, however, are core to scientific
discourseandreflectpragmaticchoicesforobjectivityandprecision.Consequently,generic
models frequently introduce semantic drift, where well-formed scientific content is simplified
orrestructuredinwaysthatcompromiseinformationalfidelity.Examplesincludereplacing
technical jargon with vague synonyms or reordering clauses in a manner that changes causal or
temporal relationships. [?]
A further domain-specific obstacle arises from LaTeX markup, which functions as a context-
sensitive typesetting language.LaTeX commands commonly involve hierarchical nesting gov-
erned by grammar rules beyond the capability of traditional regular-expression tokenizers. Neu-
raltokenizersmayfragmentcommandssuchas\frac{a}{b}intounrelatedsub-tokens(,
9
frac, {a}, /, {b}), leading to catastrophic “correction” behaviors including token deletion,
brace mismatch, or operator substitution.Such errors not only degrade semantic accuracy but
also generate compilation failures, making the manuscript unusable. [?]
To address these constraints, scientific GEC requires a pipeline architecture that integrates
linguistic conservatism with domain-aware structural protection.The system proposed in this
workintroducesadedicatedLaTeXmaskingmodulethatidentifiesmathematicalenviron-
ments(inlineanddisplaymodes)usingrecursivepatternmatchingandreplacesthemwith
immutableplaceholdersduringcorrection.Thisensures100%preservationofmathematical
content throughout the inference lifecycle. [?]
Thecorrectionstageemploysahybridneuralstrategy.Aminimal-editsequence-labeling
model (e.g., GECToR) performs deterministic local transformations to handle core grammatical
violations (article usage, morphology, agreement) while remaining highly conservative toward
sentencestructure.Inparallel,alargeinstruction-tunedgenerativemodel(e.g.,CoEdIT)is
engaged only for clauses containing complex error patterns requiring structural rephrasing.A
weighted filtering mechanism governs its influence to prevent overcorrection. [?]
Finally,asemanticrerankingmoduleutilizingSciBERT-basedperplexityscoringandco-
sine similarity to scientific embeddings evaluates multiple candidate outputs.This component
determines whether modifications introduce lexical deviation or meaning shift beyond thresh-
old tolerance.Candidate sentences with strong semantic alignment to the source and syntactic
normalization are selected as final outputs.

ThroughthisintegratedworkflowLaTeX-awaremasking,hybridconstrainedcorrection,
andsemanticvalidation,thesystemmaintainsabalancebetweenlinguisticenhancementand
scientific integrity.The resulting corrections improve readability, coherence, and grammatical-
ity without compromising the mathematical technical precision critical in engineering manuscripts.

Chapter 4
Literature Review
This complete literature review provides a comprehensive comparative analysis of twenty-four
academic writing and grammar-assistance tools, that detail their origins, underlying technolo-
gies, functional capabilities, limitations and faults.These tools include widely recognized plat-
formssuchasGrammarly,ProWritingAid,QuillBot,Ginger,andLanguageTool,alongwith
academic-focused tools like Trinka, Paperpal, Writefull, and SciSpace.
Together, they illustrate the current technological landscape of writing support systems used
bystudents,researchers,andprofessionalwriters.Thetoolsdifferlargelyintheirtechnical
foundations as some rely on large language models and transformer-based architectures, while
others use hybrid approaches that combine rule-based and machine-learning components, and a
few operate almost entirely through classical rule-based NLP. This diversity reflects an evolving
field that is shifting from deterministic grammar checking to more context-aware and semanti-
cally sensitive AI-driven writing enhancement. [?]
Scientificandengineeringtextspresentuniquechallengesforgrammaticalerrorcorrec-
tion (GEC). Unlike general writing, these documents contain dense terminology, long syntactic
structures, and non-natural language tokens such as equations, pseudo-code, algorithm descrip-
tions, and citation formats. [?]
Acrossalltoolsresearched,abroadrangeoferrortypesisdetected,includinggrammar,
punctuation, spelling, readability, clarity, style, tone, and wordiness.Many also offer advanced
academic functions such as formal tone preservation, scholarly phrasing suggestions, vocabu-
lary enhancement, and real-time structural feedback.Some platforms provide domain-specific
featurestailoredtoacademicwriting,suchasscientificterminologyrefinement,journal-style

such as published research articles.

cializedacademicgrammarcorrectionprojectcapableofaddressingweaknessesthatcurrent

Chapter 5
Problem Statement
Scientific and engineering documents frequently contain complex grammatical structures, domain-
specific terminology,and mathematical expressions written in LaTeX. Existing grammar cor-
rection systems often improve linguistic fluency but fail to preserve the integrity of embedded
equationsandsymbolicnotation.Evenminorcorruptionofmathematicalexpressions,such
asalteringE=mc2totextualform,renderstheoutputunusableforacademicandscientific
purposes.
Additionally,traditionalcorrectionapproachesrelyonsingle-modelarchitecturesthatat-
tempt to rewrite entire sentences.These models are computationally expensive, prone to hallu-
cinations, and unable to efficiently handle large documents containing section-specific writing
styles and long-range dependencies.
There is therefore a need for a methodology that:
•Corrects grammatical and stylistic issues in scientific writing while
•Fully preserving LaTeX expressions and mathematical content, and
•Scales efficiently across long technical documents without altering meaning.
The problem addressed in this work is the design of a hybrid correction pipeline that com-
binesrecursivemaskingandtokenizationtoprotectLaTeXregions,adual-modelarchitec-
ture (Sequence-to-Sequence rewriting and Sequence Tagging) to perform high-quality correc-
tion, semantic reranking to prevent hallucination, and post-edit reconstruction to ensure exact
restoration of mathematical notation.
17
Theobjectiveistoproduceacorrectionsystemthatimprovesgrammaticalaccuracyand
claritywithoutmodifyingthescientificintent,formatting,ormathematicalequationspresent
in the original text.
18
Chapter 6
Objectives
The aim of this project is to develop a writing assistant that enhances the grammatical accuracy
and lexical precision of scientific manuscripts while preserving the researcher’s original style
andensuringcompliancewithacademicintegritystandards.Toachievethis,theprojectsets
out the following specific objectives:
1.Develop a Hybrid Scientific Grammar Correction System
Scientificwritingoftencontainscomplexsentencestructures,discipline-specifictermi-
nology, and long-range dependencies.To handle these effectively, the project combines:
•A Sequence-to-Sequence model (CoEdIT) to perform deeper grammatical restruc-
turing and improve fluency
•A Sequence-Tagging model (GECToR) to perform quick and conservative token-
level corrections
This hybrid approach enables both accuracy and efficiency, ensuring the system corrects
nuanced scientific language while remaining suitable for real-time deployment.
2.Preserve Scientific Meaning Through Semantic Verification
Any modification that alters scientific claims could misrepresent results or lead to misin-
formation.
To prevent this, the system implements:
•BERTScore-based semantic similarity evaluation
19
•Pseudo-perplexity scoring to detect unnatural, incoherent rewrites
Corrections are only accepted if they remain faithful to the author’s intended meaning.If
semantic shifts are detected, the system either falls back to safer corrections or keeps the
original text unchanged.
3.Maintain the Integrity of LaTeX Structures and Research Formatting
Research manuscripts include mathematical expressions,references,section labels,fig-
ures, and tables.Such components must remain untouched.Therefore, the system:
•Extracts and masks LaTeX environments during processing
•Restores them accurately afterward
•Ensures zero loss of formatting or equation correctness
This allows text-only processing while safeguarding critical academic elements.
4.Adapt Editing to Section-Specific Scientific Writing Norms
Different sections of a research paper follow different linguistic conventions.Examples:
•Past tense in Methodology
•Present tense for established knowledge in Introduction
•Objective tone in Results and Discussion
Byincorporatingstructuredmetadata(e.g.,throughGROBID),thesystemappliescor-
rections that maintain these discipline-specific writing rules, resulting in improved con-
sistency and professionalism.
5.Follow a Precision-First Editing Policy
Thesystemaimstobuildtrustamongresearchersbyfocusingoncorrectionaccuracy
instead of aggression.Therefore:
•Only unambiguous grammatical errors are corrected
20
•Stylistic rewriting and paraphrasing are limited
•Authorial tone and sentence structure are respected
This approach avoids “over-editing” and reinforces academic integrity standards.
6.Improve Readability, Coherence, and Terminology Accuracy
In addition to grammar correction, the system supports better communication by:
•Recommending clearer or more precise terminology
•Resolving punctuation, article usage, and agreement errors
•Promoting consistent vocabulary usage across the manuscript
The end result is a scientifically polished document that is easier to read and understand.

7.1LaTeX Preservation Architecture
In the domain of scientific engineering, the preservation of mathematical notation is paramount.
2A GEC system that corrects ”the data is” to ”the data are” but corrupts E=mcto E equals
mc squared is functionally useless.We introduce a robust architecture for LaTeX preservation
centered on a ”Mask-Correct-Unmask” paradigm.
7.1.1The Regex-Based Masking Strategy
ThefirstlineofdefenseisidentifyingLaTeXsegments.Whilesimpleregexes(e.g.,$.*?$)
work for basic inline math, they fail on nested structures common in engineering papers, such
asif x > 0 .
Recursive Pattern Matching
Standard Regular Grammars cannot parse nested structures. However, the PCRE (Perl Compat-
ible Regular Expressions) engine supports recursion.In Python, the regex library (a superset of
the standard re) enables this.We define a recursive pattern to capture balanced LaTeX braces:
Python Code
#ConceptualRecursivePatternforLaTeXEnvironments
pattern=r’\\begin\{([a-z]+)\}((?:[ˆ{}]|(?R))*)\\end\{\1\}’

7.1.2Sentinel Tokenization and Embedding
Once identified, LaTeX segments must be hidden from the GEC model.We replace the iden-
tifiedsegmentwithaplaceholder.Thechoiceofplaceholderismathematicallysignificant
regarding the model’s embedding space.
The Soft-Masking Problem
If the mask token belongs to the model’s vocabulary, the embedding layer may inject contextual
bias.This can distort nearby corrections or even modify the LaTeX itself.
The Unique Alphanumeric Hash Strategy
Tocombatthis,wehasheachmaskedsegmentatinferencetimeusingacryptographichash
such as SHA-256.Because each placeholder is unique and out-of-vocabulary, the model treats
it as an inert token, eliminating semantic drift.
7.1.3Post-Editing Reconstruction
After correction,hashes are replaced by their corresponding original LaTeX segments from a
stored lookup table.This ensures full restoration without corruption or hallucinated modifica-
tions.

7.4.1Candidate Generation and Evaluation
Both GECToR and CoEdIT produce candidate corrections.These are then evaluated relative to

Sentence-BERT Similarity Scoring
WecomputepairwisesemanticsimilarityscoresusingSentence-BERTembeddings.Candi-

7.5Stage 4:Reconstruction of Mathematical Structures
After linguistic corrections are validated, the system reintroduces all mathematical and LaTeX

7.5.1Hash-Based Lookup Restoration
Each SHA-256 sentinel token is mapped back to its original LaTeX expression using a deter-
ministic lookup table constructed during Stage 1.
7.5.2Zero-Corruption Reintegration
Because the correction models never operated on LaTeX tokens directly, reconstruction is loss-
less.Allequations,environments,symbols,andmacrosarerestoredexactlyasauthored,en-
suring:
•zero corruption of mathematical content,
•full compilability of LaTeX documents,
•complete separation between linguistic edits and technical notation.
The resulting text is grammatically improved while remaining mathematically pristine.

Chapter 8
Implementation, Result, Discussion
8.1System Architecture Overview
Thefinalsystemwasimplementedasamodular,multi-stagecorrectionpipelineinGoogle
Colab, integrating:
1.A lightweight sequence tagger for fast surface-level grammar fixes
2.A Transformer-based Seq2Seq model for deep rewrites
3.A semantic reranker ensuring meaning preservation

Chapter 1
Abstract
The process begins by ingesting the source document, typically a PDF, into a structural parser.
We will utilize GROBID, a machine learning library specifically designed for scholarly articles,
to deconstruct the document into a structured format. This initial step is critical as it identifies
and tags the constituent parts of the paper, such as the abstract, introduction, methods, and
results sections. The output is the document’s text enriched with sectional metadata, which is
vital for later stages, as grammatical and stylistic conventions (e.g., tense usage) vary signifi-
cantly across these sections. The core of both models is the self-attention mechanism, which
enables the network to compute contextual relationships across the entire sentence. This is
especially useful for scientific text where long-range dependencies frequently occur (e.g., sub-
jects separated from verbs by complex noun phrases). Using scaled dot-product attention, each
token representation is updated by weighting its relevance with respect to all other tokens, while
multi-head attention enables simultaneous extraction of syntactic and semantic features. This
ensures that corrections respect both domain terminology and sentence structure. For complex
grammatical rewriting, the system employs a transformer-based encoder-decoder model such
as CoEdIT. The model treats error correction as a conditional text generation task, producing
corrected output token-by-token using learned linguistic patterns. [?]
Instruction tuning allows control over the style of edits such as grammatical fixing or formal
language enhancement which is crucial for maintaining the scientific tone. Relative positional
embeddings further improve the model’s ability to handle long sentences commonly found in
academic manuscripts. For faster, high-confidence adjustments, the system utilizes GECToR,
an encoder-only architecture that predicts token-level edit tags instead of regenerating the entire
7
sentence. These tags (e.g., KEEP, DELETE, REPLACE) enable minimal, targeted corrections
without risking loss of meaning. The model iteratively refines its output, ensuring consistency
across dependent grammatical fixes. Due to linear inference complexity, this component runs
efficiently in real-time environments such as Google Colab. The final output is produced by
intelligently combining the strengths of both models generative corrections from CoEdIT for
complex grammatical restructuring and fast tagging-based modifications from GECToR for
local edits. This hybrid strategy balances accuracy and computational efficiency while main-
taining the author’s original scientific intent.
8
Chapter 2
Introduction
Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) in scientific domains is very different from general gram-
mar correction. Scientific and engineering papers contain many technical terms, detailed sen-
tence structures, and elements like equations or LaTeX commands that are not part of nor-
mal language. Scientific and engineering manuscripts exhibit a much higher concentration of
domain-specific terminology, complex syntactic structures, and non-natural language tokens.
These include mathematical equations, chemical expressions, algorithmic pseudo-code, fig-
ure/table references, and structured citation formats (e.g., IEEE, ACM). Such constructs carry
high semantic load and must be preserved verbatim. Any unintended modification risks altering
experimental claims, mathematical correctness, or reproducibility.
General-purpose GEC models, typically trained on learner corpora or web text, optimize for
fluency gains and penalize scientific writing characteristics such as passive voice, nominaliza-
tions, and discontinuous noun phrases. These stylistic patterns, however, are core to scientific
discourse and reflect pragmatic choices for objectivity and precision. Consequently, generic
models frequently introduce semantic drift, where well-formed scientific content is simplified
or restructured in ways that compromise informational fidelity. Examples include replacing
technical jargon with vague synonyms or reordering clauses in a manner that changes causal or
temporal relationships. [?]
A further domain-specific obstacle arises from LaTeX markup, which functions as a context-
sensitive typesetting language. LaTeX commands commonly involve hierarchical nesting gov-
erned by grammar rules beyond the capability of traditional regular-expression tokenizers. Neu-
ral tokenizers may fragment commands such as \frac{a}{b} into unrelated sub-tokens (,
9
frac, {a}, /, {b}), leading to catastrophic “correction” behaviors including token deletion,
brace mismatch, or operator substitution. Such errors not only degrade semantic accuracy but
also generate compilation failures, making the manuscript unusable. [?]
To address these constraints, scientific GEC requires a pipeline architecture that integrates
linguistic conservatism with domain-aware structural protection. The system proposed in this
work introduces a dedicated LaTeX masking module that identifies mathematical environ-
ments (inline and display modes) using recursive pattern matching and replaces them with
immutable placeholders during correction. This ensures 100% preservation of mathematical
content throughout the inference lifecycle. [?]
The correction stage employs a hybrid neural strategy. A minimal-edit sequence-labeling
model (e.g., GECToR) performs deterministic local transformations to handle core grammatical
violations (article usage, morphology, agreement) while remaining highly conservative toward
sentence structure. In parallel, a large instruction-tuned generative model (e.g., CoEdIT) is
engaged only for clauses containing complex error patterns requiring structural rephrasing. A
weighted filtering mechanism governs its influence to prevent overcorrection. [?]
Finally, a semantic reranking module utilizing SciBERT-based perplexity scoring and co-
sine similarity to scientific embeddings evaluates multiple candidate outputs. This component
determines whether modifications introduce lexical deviation or meaning shift beyond thresh-
old tolerance. Candidate sentences with strong semantic alignment to the source and syntactic
normalization are selected as final outputs.
[?]
Through this integrated workflow LaTeX-aware masking, hybrid constrained correction,
and semantic validation, the system maintains a balance between linguistic enhancement and
scientific integrity. The resulting corrections improve readability, coherence, and grammatical-
ity without compromising the mathematical technical precision critical in engineering manuscripts.
10
Chapter 3
Motivation
The motivation behind this project arises from the increasing demand for a domain-specific
grammar correction system tailored to scientific and engineering manuscripts. In research
communication, every detail whether a technical term, mathematical expression, or specific
sentence structure carries precise meaning. Even minor grammatical errors or unclear phrasing
can lead to misinterpretation of experimental results and negatively impact the credibility of the
work. Moreover, many scientific papers undergo rigorous editorial and peer-review processes,
where clarity, correctness, and consistency in language are essential for successful publication.
However, existing AI-based writing assistants are primarily designed for general-purpose
text improvement. These tools often focus on making sentences sound more fluent or “natural,”
which can unintentionally modify the author’s intended technical meaning. They also struggle
to correctly handle specialized content such as LaTeX equations, algorithmic pseudo-code,
and field-specific terminology. When such elements are mistakenly changed or removed, the
document may become scientifically inaccurate or fail to compile entirely.
These limitations highlight the need for a grammar correction system that prioritizes accu-
racy over aggressive rewriting. The intended tool should correct grammatical issues, punctua-
tion errors, and stylistic inconsistencies while preserving the author’s original scientific content
and writing style. A core objective is to avoid semantic drift and ensure that meaningful scien-
tific details remain unchanged.
This project is motivated by the broader goal of supporting researchers, students, and tech-
nical professionals in producing well-structured and publication-ready scientific documents.
By delivering corrections that maintain mathematical fidelity and technical integrity, the sys-
11
tem enables authors to focus on their research outcomes rather than struggling with linguistic
challenges.
12
Chapter 4
Literature Review
This complete literature review provides a comprehensive comparative analysis of twenty-four
academic writing and grammar-assistance tools, that detail their origins, underlying technolo-
gies, functional capabilities, limitations and faults. These tools include widely recognized plat-
forms such as Grammarly, ProWritingAid, QuillBot, Ginger, and LanguageTool, along with
academic-focused tools like Trinka, Paperpal, Writefull, and SciSpace.
Together, they illustrate the current technological landscape of writing support systems used
by students, researchers, and professional writers. The tools differ largely in their technical
foundations as some rely on large language models and transformer-based architectures, while
others use hybrid approaches that combine rule-based and machine-learning components, and a
few operate almost entirely through classical rule-based NLP. This diversity reflects an evolving
field that is shifting from deterministic grammar checking to more context-aware and semanti-
cally sensitive AI-driven writing enhancement. [?]
Scientific and engineering texts present unique challenges for grammatical error correc-
tion (GEC). Unlike general writing, these documents contain dense terminology, long syntactic
structures, and non-natural language tokens such as equations, pseudo-code, algorithm descrip-
tions, and citation formats. [?]
Across all tools researched, a broad range of error types is detected, including grammar,
punctuation, spelling, readability, clarity, style, tone, and wordiness. Many also offer advanced
academic functions such as formal tone preservation, scholarly phrasing suggestions, vocabu-
lary enhancement, and real-time structural feedback. Some platforms provide domain-specific
features tailored to academic writing, such as scientific terminology refinement, journal-style
13
consistency checks, integration with LaTeX workflows, or access to discipline-specific corpora,
such as published research articles.
However, despite these strengths, most tools consistently struggle with the complexities of
scientific and technical writing. They often misinterpret domain-specific vocabulary, mishan-
dle mathematical expressions and citations, or fail to maintain semantic coherence in longer
research paragraphs. Several tools also cause issues such as over-correction, where technical
precision is often not given priority or provide suggestions that dilute academic tone or al-
ter meaning. Premium features are frequently paywalled, and transparency regarding model
decisions remains limited across many platforms.
This review identifies substantial research gaps that existing tools have not addressed appro-
priately. A major gap is the lack of domain-aware editing tailored to STEM disciplines; most
tools are not trained on scientific corpora, and therefore perform poorly on specialized terminol-
ogy and context-heavy academic sentences. Another issue is inadequate handling of long-form
scholarly writing, where tools fail to preserve logical flow, argumentation, and paragraph-level
coherence. Tools also struggle with citation-intensive writing, often breaking references or
misinterpreting structured academic syntax. Additionally, almost all tools lack transparency
and explainability, making it difficult for users, especially academic writers, to understand why
specific suggestions are made or how corrections affect meaning. Collectively, these limita-
tions suggest that while many writing assistance systems exist, none fully meet the rigorous
demands of scientific manuscript preparation. [?]
Overall, this highlights a clear opportunity for creating a new, domain-specific grammar
correction and academic writing enhancement system. A tool that would integrate scientific
terminology awareness, maintain technical precision, support long-form reasoning structures,
handle citations and mathematical expressions robustly, and provide transparent, well-justified
suggestions. The documented gaps strongly reinforce the motivation behind developing a spe-
cialized academic grammar correction project capable of addressing weaknesses that current
commercial and research tools have not resolved.
14

Commercial grammar tools such as Grammarly, ProWritingAid, QuillBot, Trinka AI, and Pa-
perpal dominate the market. They offer useful features for readability and clarity, yet none
provide a minimal-edit approach tailored for scientific writing. Most tools are generative, risk-
ing authorship concerns and potential AI detection by academic publishers.

4.2
Key Insights
Three consistent findings emerge from the literature:
4.2.1
Generative AI Risks
Generative tools excel at paraphrasing, but this presents risks for researchers. They may unin-
tentionally trigger plagiarism detectors, rewrite technical concepts, or change authorial voice.
15
These issues are widely documented in academic discussions of AI writing tools.
4.2.2
Lack of Minimal-Edit Philosophy
No reviewed tool prioritizes grammatical correction without rewriting. Literature emphasizes
a gap for systems that maintain author intent with minimal changes [?]. Academic writing
requires precision rather than stylistic fluency.
4.2.3
Technical Content Remains Unprotected
Existing solutions provide no guarantee for LaTeX equations, figures, tables, or structured
scientific notation. This absence is highlighted in work on LaTeX editing. Preservation of
mathematical structure is essential for engineering manuscripts.
4.3
Conclusion
Current grammar correction tools are optimized for general writing and generative enhance-
ments, not scientific integrity. Literature consistently identifies the need for a correction-first
methodology that preserves authorial ownership and mathematical structure. This creates a
clear research gap for solutions implementing minimal edits, LaTeX-aware masking, and se-
mantic validation that ensures clarity without compromising correctness.
16
Chapter 5
Problem Statement
Scientific and engineering documents frequently contain complex grammatical structures, domain-
specific terminology, and mathematical expressions written in LaTeX. Existing grammar cor-
rection systems often improve linguistic fluency but fail to preserve the integrity of embedded
equations and symbolic notation. Even minor corruption of mathematical expressions, such
as altering E = mc2 to textual form, renders the output unusable for academic and scientific
purposes.
Additionally, traditional correction approaches rely on single-model architectures that at-
tempt to rewrite entire sentences. These models are computationally expensive, prone to hallu-
cinations, and unable to efficiently handle large documents containing section-specific writing
styles and long-range dependencies.
There is therefore a need for a methodology that:
• Corrects grammatical and stylistic issues in scientific writing while
• Fully preserving LaTeX expressions and mathematical content, and
• Scales efficiently across long technical documents without altering meaning.
The problem addressed in this work is the design of a hybrid correction pipeline that com-
bines recursive masking and tokenization to protect LaTeX regions, a dual-model architec-
ture (Sequence-to-Sequence rewriting and Sequence Tagging) to perform high-quality correc-
tion, semantic reranking to prevent hallucination, and post-edit reconstruction to ensure exact
restoration of mathematical notation.
17
The objective is to produce a correction system that improves grammatical accuracy and
clarity without modifying the scientific intent, formatting, or mathematical equations present
in the original text.
18
Chapter 6
Objectives
The aim of this project is to develop a writing assistant that enhances the grammatical accuracy
and lexical precision of scientific manuscripts while preserving the researcher’s original style
and ensuring compliance with academic integrity standards. To achieve this, the project sets
out the following specific objectives:
1. Develop a Hybrid Scientific Grammar Correction System
Scientific writing often contains complex sentence structures, discipline-specific termi-
nology, and long-range dependencies. To handle these effectively, the project combines:
• A Sequence-to-Sequence model (CoEdIT) to perform deeper grammatical restruc-
turing and improve fluency
• A Sequence-Tagging model (GECToR) to perform quick and conservative token-
level corrections
This hybrid approach enables both accuracy and efficiency, ensuring the system corrects
nuanced scientific language while remaining suitable for real-time deployment.
2. Preserve Scientific Meaning Through Semantic Verification
Any modification that alters scientific claims could misrepresent results or lead to misin-
formation.
To prevent this, the system implements:
• BERTScore-based semantic similarity evaluation
19
• Pseudo-perplexity scoring to detect unnatural, incoherent rewrites
Corrections are only accepted if they remain faithful to the author’s intended meaning. If
semantic shifts are detected, the system either falls back to safer corrections or keeps the
original text unchanged.
3. Maintain the Integrity of LaTeX Structures and Research Formatting
Research manuscripts include mathematical expressions, references, section labels, fig-
ures, and tables. Such components must remain untouched. Therefore, the system:
• Extracts and masks LaTeX environments during processing
• Restores them accurately afterward
• Ensures zero loss of formatting or equation correctness
This allows text-only processing while safeguarding critical academic elements.
4. Adapt Editing to Section-Specific Scientific Writing Norms
Different sections of a research paper follow different linguistic conventions. Examples:
• Past tense in Methodology
• Present tense for established knowledge in Introduction
• Objective tone in Results and Discussion
By incorporating structured metadata (e.g., through GROBID), the system applies cor-
rections that maintain these discipline-specific writing rules, resulting in improved con-
sistency and professionalism.
5. Follow a Precision-First Editing Policy
The system aims to build trust among researchers by focusing on correction accuracy
instead of aggression. Therefore:
• Only unambiguous grammatical errors are corrected
20
• Stylistic rewriting and paraphrasing are limited
• Authorial tone and sentence structure are respected
This approach avoids “over-editing” and reinforces academic integrity standards.
6. Improve Readability, Coherence, and Terminology Accuracy
In addition to grammar correction, the system supports better communication by:
• Recommending clearer or more precise terminology
• Resolving punctuation, article usage, and agreement errors
• Promoting consistent vocabulary usage across the manuscript
The end result is a scientifically polished document that is easier to read and understand.
21
Chapter 7
Proposed Method
7.1
LaTeX Preservation Architecture
In the domain of scientific engineering, the preservation of mathematical notation is paramount.
A GEC system that corrects ”the data is” to ”the data are” but corrupts E = mc2 to E equals
mc squared is functionally useless. We introduce a robust architecture for LaTeX preservation
centered on a ”Mask-Correct-Unmask” paradigm.
7.1.1
The Regex-Based Masking Strategy
The first line of defense is identifying LaTeX segments. While simple regexes (e.g., $.*?$)
work for basic inline math, they fail on nested structures common in engineering papers, such
as if x > 0 .
Recursive Pattern Matching
Standard Regular Grammars cannot parse nested structures. However, the PCRE (Perl Compat-
ible Regular Expressions) engine supports recursion. In Python, the regex library (a superset of
the standard re) enables this. We define a recursive pattern to capture balanced LaTeX braces:
Python Code
# Conceptual Recursive Pattern for LaTeX Environments
pattern = r’\\begin\{([a-z]+)\}((?:[ˆ{}]|(?R))*)\\end\{\1\}’
Here, (?R) invokes the pattern recursively, allowing the parser to handle arbitrarily deep
nesting of braces, ensuring that an environment like
a
b
(7.1)
22
is captured as a single atomic unit.
7.1.2
Sentinel Tokenization and Embedding
Once identified, LaTeX segments must be hidden from the GEC model. We replace the iden-
tified segment with a placeholder. The choice of placeholder is mathematically significant
regarding the model’s embedding space.
The Soft-Masking Problem
If the mask token belongs to the model’s vocabulary, the embedding layer may inject contextual
bias. This can distort nearby corrections or even modify the LaTeX itself.
The Unique Alphanumeric Hash Strategy
To combat this, we hash each masked segment at inference time using a cryptographic hash
such as SHA-256. Because each placeholder is unique and out-of-vocabulary, the model treats
it as an inert token, eliminating semantic drift.
7.1.3
Post-Editing Reconstruction
After correction, hashes are replaced by their corresponding original LaTeX segments from a
stored lookup table. This ensures full restoration without corruption or hallucinated modifica-
tions.
7.2
Stage 2: Hybrid Neural Correction Architecture
Once LaTeX content is safely masked, the remaining natural language is processed using a
dual-lane hybrid correction approach. This architecture combines the precision of sequence
tagging with the expressive power of transformer-based editing, ensuring minimal hallucination
while enabling structural improvements.
7.2.1
Fast Lane: GECToR-Based Sequence Tagging
GECToR operates through token-level edit tags such as KEEP, REPLACE, and DELETE. This
enables highly localized, deterministic corrections.
23
Deterministic Micro-Corrections
GECToR is responsible for:
• article and determiner corrections,
• subject–verb agreement repair,
• punctuation and capitalization fixes,
• minor phrase-level restructuring.
Because sequence tagging constrains the model to minimal edits, the risk of hallucination
or semantic drift is negligible.
7.2.2
Deep Lane: CoEdIT Sequence-to-Sequence Editor
For sentences requiring major structural improvements, a second, more expressive model is
used.
Instruction-Tuned Structural Rewriting
CoEdIT is a transformer-based seq2seq model specifically trained for controlled text improve-
ment. Unlike generic LLMs, which may introduce new information, CoEdIT is optimized for:
• formal tone enhancement,
• clause reordering,
• syntactic disambiguation,
• elimination of awkward or ambiguous phrasing.
This two-lane strategy ensures both efficiency and linguistic depth, while safeguarding
against semantic alteration.
24
7.3
Stage 2: Hybrid Neural Correction Architecture
Once LaTeX content is safely masked, the remaining natural language is processed using a
dual-lane hybrid correction approach. This architecture combines the precision of sequence
tagging with the expressive power of transformer-based editing, ensuring minimal hallucination
while enabling structural improvements.

used.
Instruction-Tuned Structural Rewriting
CoEdIT is a transformer-based seq2seq model specifically trained for controlled text improve-

• formal tone enhancement,
25
• clause reordering,
• syntactic disambiguation,
• elimination of awkward or ambiguous phrasing.
This two-lane strategy ensures both efficiency and linguistic depth, while safeguarding
against semantic alteration.
7.4
Stage 3: Semantic Reranking (Meaning Preservation En-
gine)
Neural correction models may generate multiple plausible rewrites. However, the key con-
straint of scientific editing is that the meaning must remain unchanged. To enforce this, we
integrate a semantic fidelity module that filters and ranks candidates.
7.4.1
Candidate Generation and Evaluation
Both GECToR and CoEdIT produce candidate corrections. These are then evaluated relative to
the original sentence.
Sentence-BERT Similarity Scoring
We compute pairwise semantic similarity scores using Sentence-BERT embeddings. Candi-
dates exhibiting strong semantic alignment are prioritized, while those deviating from the orig-
inal meaning are penalized.
7.4.2
Semantic Drift Rejection
If a candidate falls below a similarity threshold, it is discarded outright. The system then
defaults to a more conservative correction (typically GECToR), ensuring that:
• no factual content is altered,
• no claims or numerical values are modified,
• no scientific statements are softened or strengthened unintentionally.
26
This mechanism serves as a “meaning preservation firewall” that guarantees scientific in-
tegrity.
7.5
Stage 4: Reconstruction of Mathematical Structures
After linguistic corrections are validated, the system reintroduces all mathematical and LaTeX
content previously masked.
7.5.1
Hash-Based Lookup Restoration
Each SHA-256 sentinel token is mapped back to its original LaTeX expression using a deter-
ministic lookup table constructed during Stage 1.
7.5.2
Zero-Corruption Reintegration
Because the correction models never operated on LaTeX tokens directly, reconstruction is loss-
less. All equations, environments, symbols, and macros are restored exactly as authored, en-
suring:
• zero corruption of mathematical content,
• full compilability of LaTeX documents,
• complete separation between linguistic edits and technical notation.
The resulting text is grammatically improved while remaining mathematically pristine.
27
7.6
Multi-Stage Pipeline Design
The proposed solution for Google Colab is structured as a cascading pipeline. This design
optimizes the trade-off between the computational cost of LLMs and the speed of sequence
taggers, while ensuring robustness via semantic reranking.
7.6.1
Stage 0: Parsing and Section Identification
The input text provided by the user is first parsed to identify the structural sections of the re-
search paper (e.g., Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion). Section headers,
LaTeX commands, tables, and figures are detected and tagged to ensure that downstream cor-
rection respects the document’s structure.
28
7.6.2
Stage 1: Preprocessing and Segmentation
Input text is segmented into sentences and tokenized. Domain-specific errors such as unit
formatting or equation references are flagged early.
7.6.3
Stage 2: Hybrid Neural Correction
A fast Sequence Tagging model performs deterministic error tagging while a Transformer-
based Seq2Seq model handles more complex rewrites. Outputs are combined using confidence
scoring.
7.6.4
Stage 3: Semantic Reranking
Multiple candidate outputs are generated. A sentence-level encoder (e.g., Sentence-BERT)
computes semantic similarity scores and ranks them based on grammatical confidence and
semantic preservation.
7.6.5
Stage 4: Reconstruction
The corrected text and preserved LaTeX tokens are recombined. Formatting and whitespace
consistency are validated to ensure publication-ready output.
29

CoEdIT vs. GPT-4
GPT-4 performs well on general language tasks, but in scientific writing it often rewrites sen-

Chapter 9
Conclusion and Future Work
This section presents the preliminary results and functional analysis of our grammar correction
tool. The findings are organized sequentially, corresponding to the distinct stages of our pro-
posed data processing pipeline. These results serve as a proof-of-concept, demonstrating the
successful implementation of the foundational components and validating the overall architec-
tural design. The aim is to create a comprehensive, reliable, and integrity-conscious writing
assistant.
Evaluating grammar correction systems for scientific text is challenging because standard
metrics do not reflect scientific correctness. We therefore combine quantitative metrics with a
domain-specific measure of LaTeX preservation.
9.1
Dataset Metrics
To evaluate the grammar correction pipeline, a synthetic, domain-specific dataset was created
to simulate academic and scientific writing errors. The dataset was designed to cover a wide
variety of edge cases, including:
1. LaTeX expressions and math environments
2. Units and measurements (e.g., kg, m/s)
3. Abbreviations and citations
4. Tense, agreement, and plurals
5. Nested clauses and complex sentence structures
6. Dataset Creation Process
Seed examples: 50 high-quality, manually curated examples were created to capture diverse
32
error types.
Programmatic generation: Using a Python script, the seed templates were permuted to
generate a total of 1,000 unique examples suitable for evaluation.
Validation: Each example was checked to ensure correctness, proper LaTeX formatting,
and semantic consistency with scientific text.

rected Output.
9.2
Quantitative Metrics
We report two metrics commonly used in grammatical error correction (GEC):
• GLEU (Generalized LEU): Measures n-gram overlap between the system output and
reference corrections. It correlates better with human judgment than BLEU for editing
tasks.
• ERRANT (Error Annotation Toolkit): Classifies edits made by the model, allowing us
to inspect whether it mainly fixes punctuation and agreement (desired) or modifies nouns
and verbs (undesirable).
These metrics provide a quantitative view of model behavior on scientific writing.
9.3
Qualitative Metrics
Analysis: The results in Table 9.1 demonstrate that the implemented Stage 1 engine is function-
ing as intended, fulfilling our objective to develop a high-precision GEC engine for clear-cut
errors [?]. The system successfully identifies and rectifies a range of foundational grammati-
cal issues. The processing times are well within acceptable limits for an interactive prototype.
The success of this deterministic stage, combined with the functional UI, provides a robust
foundation for the more advanced machine learning stages of the project.

Quantization and Distillation
For limited-resource environments, the notebook supports:
